quote: That's what the house got just got built, and it's not on Google Maps yet.
stewart_read: Nick's new build is a high-value signal: brand-new roof (no age concern), first utility bill not yet received (usage baseline unknown but clean slate), and a homeowner who just committed to a property and is likely thinking about long-term costs. Bucky registers the new-build context ('we just got billed') and correctly captures it for the closer — that part is fine. The actual miss is that Bucky never names this back to Nick as a curiosity hook. A new build is the ideal solar timing scenario (roof warranty intact, no retrofit complications, first bill about to arrive). Bucky had the material to say something like 'actually, new builds are the best time to look at this — your roof is fresh and you're about to see your first bill' and chose not to. Strategic hypothesis: Bucky was saving the curiosity hook for the closer. Reactive hypothesis: Bucky didn't recognize the signal's value. The reactive read holds — nothing in the call suggests Bucky was deliberately deferring this to the closer as a setup move.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky recognized the new-build signal and deliberately left the curiosity hook for the closer to deploy at the appointment.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky this moment. The new-build detail is the single richest hook in the call. Ask: 'What did you do with that?' The answer is nothing. Coach the move: name the signal back to the customer as a reason the timing is good. This doesn't require selling solar — it's one sentence that makes Nick more likely to show up tomorrow.

stewart_read: Bucky confirms Nick is on the title but never asks the required follow-up: 'Anyone else on the title?' Per verify.title_co_owners, this is marked CRITICAL for spouse coordination at button-up. If a spouse or co-owner exists and is not included in the appointment, the closer walks into a structurally broken sit. This is not a strategic omission — there is no evidence Bucky planned to surface this later. The question was simply skipped.
coaching_implication: This is the upstream cause of the appointment's structural fragility. In the 1-on-1, have Bucky recite the two-part title check: (1) confirm ownership, (2) ask 'Is anyone else on the title with you?' Make it a reflex. The closer cannot fix this gap on arrival — they'll find out mid-appointment that a spouse needs to be looped in, which kills momentum.

quote: And then you don't know how much you're paying in and all that?
stewart_read: Nick has no bill yet — he just moved in. Bucky accepts this with 'Yeah. That's fine' and moves on. The bill anchor is dropped entirely. The codex requires capture AND emotional flip; here neither happens. However, the new-build context actually creates an alternative anchor: Nick is about to receive his first electric bill on a brand-new home. That's a forward-looking pain hook ('you're about to find out what this house costs to run — that's exactly when people want to know their options'). Bucky had the material and didn't use it. Lead type is also ambiguous here — if this is a paid lead, the absence of a bill is a structural problem for the appointment; if organic, it's preferred but not required. That ambiguity is unresolved.
coaching_implication: Coach the pivot: when a customer has no bill yet, the forward-looking hook is available. 'You're about to get your first bill on a new house — that's actually the best time to look at this.' Also flag the lead-type question for the manager: if this is a paid lead, the closer needs to know there's no bill on file and should plan to collect it at the appointment.

quote: Do you have a credit score over 670?
stewart_read: Bucky states the threshold as 670, which matches the codex. Nick answers 800, then volunteers that 'next week it might be down just a little bit' — likely referencing a recent hard inquiry from the new home purchase. Bucky responds 'That's fine. That's fine.' This is procedurally acceptable but carries two unresolved issues: (1) the codex's spencer_operator_note flags that stated credit ≠ pulled credit and that setter DQ on stated credit is a policy question Kenny has not yet resolved; (2) affirming 'that's fine' on a stated 800 that the customer himself flags as potentially dropping is premature — Bucky doesn't know where it will land. The schema_drift check is clean (670 stated correctly), but the affirmation is overconfident given the open policy question.
coaching_implication: Flag the schema_drift check as clean (670 is correct). Then surface the spencer_operator_note: Bucky's 'that's fine' is probably right, but the rep is making a qualification call on stated credit that the codex explicitly marks as an unresolved policy question. Until Kenny resolves whether setters DQ on stated or defer to the closer's pull, coach Bucky to capture the answer and move on without affirming qualification — 'Got it, our specialist will confirm everything on the call tomorrow.'

quote: Alright. Sounds good. I'll text you after this to remind you, and then we'll call you 2PM tomorrow.
stewart_read: Bucky books the appointment but skips every required button-up element except the datetime and the text promise: no one-hour block stated, no computer requirement, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake, no 'not driving' anchor. Per the codex, skipping these elements raises no-show risk. The text promise is present, which is the one positive. The appointment is real but thin — Nick agreed to a time, not to a commitment.
coaching_implication: Walk Bucky through the button-up checklist against this call. He hit 2 of 6 required elements. The 'is that fair' handshake in particular is the verbal commitment that predicts show rate — it takes four words and Bucky skipped it. Have him practice the full button-up sequence until it's automatic.

quote: Do you hey. Do you have a price?
stewart_read: Nick's unsolicited price question at call's end is the strongest engagement signal in the transcript. He's curious enough to ask before hanging up. Bucky deflects correctly — 'They're gonna give you it tomorrow' — which holds setter scope and redirects to the closer. The deflection is the right move. The missed opportunity is that Bucky could have used this moment to amplify curiosity rather than just deflect: something like 'That's exactly what tomorrow's call is for — they'll build your specific design and show you the numbers for your house.' The deflection holds the line; the amplification would have strengthened the show commitment.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky's one-liner deflection was intentionally minimal to avoid scope creep.
coaching_implication: The deflection is correct — don't coach Bucky away from it. Coach the amplification layer: after deflecting, add one sentence that makes the appointment feel more valuable. 'They'll have your actual numbers' is more compelling than 'they'll give you it tomorrow.' This is a low-effort upgrade that directly improves show rate on a customer who is already curious.

quote: We just need to find you a good day to talk to a specialist.
stewart_read: Bucky moves directly from the verify checklist to booking without any curiosity-creation moment. The codex's program_pitch beat (call_shape.required_beats, order 3) is entirely absent. Nick never hears why this appointment is worth his time — no bill pain anchor (unavailable, but the forward-looking hook was available), no 'what interested you in solar' question (verify.interest_in_solar skipped entirely), no emotional alignment. The appointment is booked on compliance, not curiosity. This is the structural gap that makes the sit fragile independent of the button-up misses.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky judged that a new homeowner who filled out a form needed no additional curiosity-building and moved efficiently to booking.
coaching_implication: The strategic hypothesis is weak — there's no evidence Bucky made a deliberate judgment call here. The reactive read holds: the program pitch beat was skipped. In the 1-on-1, ask Bucky: 'What did Nick say when you asked what interested him in solar?' The answer is nothing, because Bucky never asked. That question is the emotional anchor for the entire call. Even a 30-second answer from Nick would have given Bucky material to tie back at button-up and in the text follow-up.

quote: Central Virginia something like that number.
stewart_read: Nick doesn't know his utility company by name — he just moved in. Bucky prompts with 'Is it Dominion?' and Nick corrects to 'Central Virginia Electric.' This is a minor verify gap (utility confirmed, just required a prompt) but surfaces a broader pattern: Nick is a brand-new resident with no bill, no established utility relationship, and no usage history. The closer is walking into an appointment with zero consumption data. This is not a DQ condition, but it is a data gap the closer needs to plan around.
coaching_implication: Flag for the handoff brief. The closer should expect to spend time establishing a usage baseline — either from the builder's energy estimates for the home or from Nick's first bill, which will arrive shortly. Bucky could have noted this explicitly in the text follow-up to set the closer's expectations.
